food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — braised thick wheat noodles with dark soy sauce and seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Black Hokkien Mee. FOODON_03311069 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Black Hokkien Mee. A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Black Hokkien Mee or a sufficiently close noodle dish analogue.

preparation_method: simmered in dark soy sauce broth

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Black Hokkien Mee as served in a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising thick noodles, seafood, pork, and vegetables in dark soy sauce gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Hokkien Mee (Fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Hokkien Mee (fried) as the closest approved reference. Black Hokkien Mee differs in that it uses dark soy sauce braising rather than a light prawn-stock fry, resulting in slightly higher sodium and marginally lower fat. Values are adapted from HPB Hokkien Mee (fried) per 100 g with conservative upward adjustment to sodium (~900 mg/100 g) to reflect the dark soy sauce base, consistent with published Singapore hawker food composition studies. Core macros are retained from the HPB reference as the macronutrient profile is comparable.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Hokkien Noodles and Mixed Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Hokkien Mee. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 49 for thick wheat noodle dishes (Hokkien-style noodles, boiled/braised). This value is used as the closest analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 74.0) / 100 = 36.3, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.5 × 4 = 74.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~900 mg per 100 g; ~3600 mg per standard 400 g serving) from dark soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Black Hokkien Mee to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce; a single serving can exceed the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~49) and high glycemic load per serving (~36.3 GL units) due to large portion of refined wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Black Hokkien Mee in reduced portions (200 g or less), pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high carbohydrate load per standard serving can cause significant glycaemic excursions despite the moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, moderate saturated fat from pork belly and lard, and cholesterol from seafood and pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Black Hokkien Mee due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; choosing versions prepared with vegetable oil instead of lard and requesting less sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Hokkien Mee is a Singaporean-Chinese hawker noodle dish distinguished by its rich, dark colour derived from generous amounts of dark soy sauce used during braising. Thick, round yellow wheat noodles (Hokkien noodles) are the centrepiece, cooked together with prawns, squid, sliced pork belly, fish cake, and shredded cabbage in a deeply savoury dark soy sauce gravy. The dish is typically finished with crispy pork lard bits and served with a side of sambal chilli for heat. The texture is soft and slightly sticky from the braising liquid, with a umami-forward, slightly sweet and salty flavour profile. Black Hokkien Mee is a staple of Singapore coffee shops and hawker centres, particularly popular in the Klang Valley-influenced stalls that migrated to Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~148 kcal per 100 g) with a reasonable protein contribution from seafood and pork (~7.8 g per 100 g), but it carries a very high sodium load (~900 mg per 100 g) due to the dark soy sauce base, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is moderate-low (~49) for the noodle component, but the large standard serving size (approximately 400 g) results in a high glycemic load (~36 GL units), which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains gluten (wheat noodles), shellfish, molluscs, and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation confirmation.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Black Hokkien Mee specifically. Values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Hokkien Mee (fried) reference with sodium adjusted upward to reflect the dark soy sauce braising base. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for thick wheat noodle dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Hokkien Mee (fried) per 100 g. Sodium has been conservatively adjusted upward to approximately 900 mg/100 g to reflect the dark soy sauce braising characteristic of Black Hokkien Mee, consistent with published Singapore hawker food sodium studies. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Hokkien Mee reference entry. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 8. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 9. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 10. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source. 12. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB reference; no trans fats reported in this dish category. 13. Input cuisine tag 'Filipino' is not supported by culinary evidence; Black Hokkien Mee is a Singaporean-Chinese hawker dish with Hokkien Chinese origins. Cuisine corrected to 'Singaporean-Chinese'. 14. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~49) from the University of Sydney GI Database for thick wheat noodle dishes; no direct measurement for Black Hokkien Mee was found. 15. Halal status is not confirmed as pork belly and lard are standard ingredients; is_halal set to false.
